sentences.text: Now we really come to the important things, I believe.
Here I am.
I have-- like I said a moment ago-- I have no money-- no place to go.
I have no idea what to do.
I went to my old neighborhood, to a bakery on the corner of [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE].
And the two little girls who were working there, they look at me and say, my gosh, what are you doing here?
Everybody knows you in the neighborhood!
Get out of here!
I say, I have no way.
I don't know where to go.
Then they say, well, go next door to Mr.
[INAUDIBLE], who knows you very well.
He might be able to help you.
I went to that man.
He says, get out of here!
You'll-- you'll kill yourself!
And he put on a piece of paper the address of a lady and say, you go to her.
She lives in a neighborhood where nobody knows you, and she might help you.
She lived in one of the fanciest neighborhoods and houses in Amsterdam, and I went there.
And I told her that such-and-such person sent me.
Can you help me?
She says, yes, let me make a phone call.
She made a phone call.
Then she put on a piece of paper an address and a name and said, I don't give that to you.
You have to learn it by heart.
Repeat it.
Repeat it.
And you go there, and those people will take care of you.
So I did.
I thanked her.
I left the house.
And there was a little yard in front of the house, and I walked through the yard.
And, all of a sudden, I hear a girl's voice-- Pietje!
Pietje!
My name was Peter [?
Fenstler. ?] 'Pietje'  is the diminutive.
Hold it for a moment.
And it was a little girl, maybe 18, 19 years old-- very beautiful girl.
And she said to me, I'd like to have a cup of coffee with you tomorrow.
Is that possible?
I said yes.
We made a date.
By the way, the [?
Schillerterras, ?] on Rembrandtsland-- Rembrandtsplein.
And I met her the next day.
And she said very casually to me, oh, Eric, you sh-- oh, Pietje, you should know, my mother works for the Gestapo.
Antje.
She works for the Gestapo.
And I said, what?
Yes, she works for the Gestapo.
You know, she finds hiding places for Jews and then she betrays them to the Gestapo.
I say, and you let me go there yesterday?
She says, oh, you don't have to worry.
My mother's very smart.
She always waits at least two weeks.
And so you are safe.
But I wouldn't go back.
And I say to her, well, since when is that going on?
Says, oh, since half a year, something like that.
And I says, you knew about it?
Yes, of course!
And why didn't you do anything?
She says, well, they were all old people.
I couldn't care.
But you are young and you are handsome and you're so pleasant and I-- I don't want you to get killed.
That's why I'm telling you.
She was listening to the conversation I had with her mother.
No.
The girls sent me to [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] sent me to that lady in the beautiful house, and she gave me the address where I should go.
And her daughter, Antje, he was listening to our conversations from the next room, through an open door.
And so I said to her, why is she doing that?
And she said, well, my-- my father was not Jewish.
And as you know, people who live in a mixed marriage are not to be deported-- at that time, by the way.
And since my father died, my mother is Jewish, she's not married anymore to a non-Jew, and therefore she works with the Gestapo to save her neck and save me, too.
And that was that.
Now I am again in big, big trouble and I don't know what to do.
I have no place, I have no money, and I am desperate.
I have three choices-- suicide, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] go to Gestapo and give myself up, or become a burglar or hold-up man.
But I had another idea.
It's probably not right for the tape, but I don't care at this time.
There is a very famous red-light district in Amsterdam-- very famous, very interesting.
And it was out of bounds for German army personnel.
It was very difficult for Dutch-- for German soldiers to get a girl in Holland because, contrary to France, Dutch girls did not go for German soldiers.
And they were standing at the red-light district, looking and looking.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know why.
And I had an idea.
I went into the red-- the red-light district and I went to a prostitute.
And I said to her, I am in trouble.
I have no money.
I am underground.
She asked me, why are you underground?
I said, I'm a student, and the universities require declaration of a student that he's in favor of Nazism.
And I'm not going to sign that, so I'm underground.
That, by the way, happens all the time.
And would you go with some German soldiers in one of the hotels adjacent to the red-light district?
And she said, yes, I would.
And she introduced me to another prostitute, and she did the same.
I went to German soldiers, and I talked to them, you know, and-- anyway, I made them a proposition.
Said, if they go in such-and-such hotel, I will send to them a girl-- a prostitute.
In other words, I acted as a pimp.
And I'm not ashamed of it, either.
It was unbelievable.
The one girl gave me all the money.
The other girl gave me half.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was unbelievable.
I did that for about two or three months.
And, by the way, later on, after be-- became a part of the Dutch underground resistance, I kept the whole situation up.
But not with soldiers, but with high-level officers and the girls went to the fancy hotels in Amsterdam-- Hotel [?
Amerikaan. ?] And the idea was not money.
The idea was to get some information.
Anyway, I went along for a few months.
Then, coincidentally, I met someone whom I knew from the Werkdorp, someone by the name of Kurt Reilinger, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he had a strange uniform.
It was not an SS uniform, but it looked like it.
But it wasn't.
And I went to him, and I say, Kurt!
What-- what's that?
He says, Eric, you have such a big mouth, I don't tell you, because-- He called you Eric, or Peter?
He didn't know me as Peter.
He knew me as Eric.
It was still in [?
Viering. ?] That's where he knew me from.
So he told me what he was doing.
There was an Organization Todt.
T-O-D-T.
It was a big, German construction company and they were doing fortifications at the-- to build the Atlantic Wall as a defense against a possible invasion.
And he was working for them as a guy to get Dutch laborer.
And he got lots of young people.
And they were hired by the Organization Todt-- Jews and non-Jews.
Once they came to France, they worked with the Organization Todt for a few weeks, close to the Spanish border, the Gulf of Biscay.
The Du-- The Dutch-- the non-Jewish boys-- went to Spain, and went from there to England.
The Jewish boys went from there to Spain, and went from there with [INAUDIBLE] from Spain to-- to Israel.
That's what he was doing.
And I said, I want to help you.
He said, well, I don't think you have the capability to do that.
And, well, he got me in touch with somebody else, and they tested me how good I am.
Now, there was rationing-- food rationing-- in Holland at the time, which was very, very tight.
And you needed a document, which was called 'eine stamkaarte.' And with that, you had to go to distribution offices where you got coupons for milk and butter and meat and-- very little.
And the-- all the people who lived underground, Jews and non-Jews, they had no document for food rationing.
So that one girl-- her name was Laura Doller-- Jewish girl-- she said, OK, we can use you for the following.
Here are 80 or 100-- I forgot-- 80 or 100 stamkaarten.
And you go to various distribution offices and get food rations.
But don't give anyone more than five or six.
Only five or six.
So I go to the first distribution office, you know, with my big briefcase full of stamkaarten.
I took out five, and I went there.
And there was that clerk who had the Nazi-- the Nazi party in Holland, the name was NSP.
He had the NSP emblem on his coat.
And he looked at the stamkaarte, he runs it through something, and he says, that's blocked!
I say, what does that mean, 'blocked'?
He says, never mind.
And he called an officer-- a police officer-- who was there.
The police officer came.
The Dutch Nazi said to him, that man here is trying to get coupons off-- off a stamkaarte which doesn't exist.
He's most likely in the black market.
That I was a Jew never dawned on him.
Black market.
So the police officer opened my briefcase, and there were 80-- approximately 80 stamkaarten in.
He closed it, and he said, there's nothing in it.
Everything's fine.
So then he asked me, where did you get the-- the-- the stamkaarte?
And I'm completely inexperienced.
I said, well, I go for dinner occasionally to a certain place-- a private house-- where people come for dinner together.
They are old people, and they cannot walk, and they cannot go by bicycle.
And they asked me to be so nice and get for them their food distributions.
And that's where I got it from.
And they believed it, and that was that.
And that was my first illegal activity.
And I did well.
[LAUGH]
Yeah.
Then, I had to tell one of my [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] superiors at the underground group about the Jewish lady who works with the Gestapo.
I told them the story.
And he said to me, she has to be killed.
I say, I agree.
She's a traitor.
She's dangerous.
And they said to me, you have to kill her.
And I said, I-- I cannot kill people.
It's not my job.
I cannot do that.
Then he said to me, well, who else can?
You have her confidence.
You know who she is.
You're convinced.
She died shortly thereafter.
What else?
[LAUGHTER] [INAUDIBLE]
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the meantime, I got more and more involved in all of that.
There was in Holland a camp by the name of Westerbork.
Westerbork was not a concentration camp.
Westerbork was a camp where all the people in-- the Jews-- in Holland who were arrested or had to come, they all came to Westerbork.
And they stayed there for weeks or months, depending on many circumstances.
And every Tuesday-- every Tuesday-- a train, which was called the 'transport trein,' left from Westerbork to Auschwitz or to Sobibor with about 1,000 people in it.
And Holland had 130,000 Jews when the Nazi came.
About 104,000 were deported in those two camps.
And maybe several hundred, maybe as many as 1,000, came back out of the 130,000 Jews living in Holland and that were deported.
For practical purposes-- no practical purposes-- nobody came back.
And the 30,000 Jews which still were in Holland after the war, there was 20,000 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] underground people who survived, and 10, maybe, mixed marriages or special circumstances.
I mean, those are approximate numbers.
We had a way of getting boys-- not girls-- out of Westerbork and put them into the Organization Todt de France.
That was done through an underground tunnel where people were at work.
And some people that dug-- Hanneman was the name of the guy who did it-- they-- they did a-- a little tunnel underground.
And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they went through that underground tunnel and were picked up by us.
And one day, Laura Doller and I, we took our train from Amsterdam to Assen-- that was the big city in that area.
And we had our two bicycles on the train.
And we were supposed to pick up two people-- Gerhardt Wunch and Hans Oster.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So we left the station, Assen, with our bicycles, and went towards Hooghalen.
Hooghalen is the little village which is close to the camp Westerbork.
And Laura and I, we were pedalling on our bicycles.
And we come to Hooghalen.
And I see two [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] is the same like-- it's not police, a very special police.
And in the [INAUDIBLE], there were comparatively many Nazis.
In the police department, hardly any.
But in the [INAUDIBLE] were many.
And I was very, very worried, because they looked at us like this.
And I knew there was an old German general of the 19th century.
He used to say, the best defense is attack.
So I attacked.
I went to those two police officers and asked them for-- what is the shortest way to go to Hooghalen?
And he looked at me, and he said, in Dutch, [SPEAKING DUTCH].
Which means, in English, 'You guys are foreigners from Jerusalem.' Which is an old Dutch expression which I didn't know.
But when he said, you are foreigners from Jerusalem, I thought, my gosh, the son of a bitch knew we are Jews and now we are dead.
But he explained the way to me and I told Laura what he said.
And she started to laugh and explained to me, there's an old Dutch expression.
Anyway, we pedaled with our two bicycles to a certain place near to the camp.
You could see the camp and could see people work.
And out of the bushes, Hans Oster and Gerhardt Wunch came out.
And we put them in front of our bicycle, which is not unusual.
And we drove, pedaling only about three, four, kilometers because somewhere else, two bicycles for them were kept.
And then we went to Amsterdam.
And they went, later on, through Organization Todt, to-- to Israel.
And that was actually the only time that I was personally involved, directly, in taking two human beings out of the camp.
I mean, I was involved indirectly all the time, but that is the only [BACKGROUND NOISE] personal activity I had.
And I think's kind of interesting.
And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] something else very, very interesting.
We are now in December, '43.
December, '43.
I was asked, why don't you go to-- also to the Organization Todt and-- and escape to Spain?
I say, no, I want to stay here.
I'm needed here.
I want to stay here.
Well, to make a long story short, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the following situation happened.
Kurt Reilinger said to me, you know, the next group-- those little transports through the south of France-- they were usually not more than six or eight guys.
He says, you know, we have a very interesting situation.
We have a German national by the name of Biedermann, and he is a deserter of the German army.
And we have complete confidence in him.
We are going to take him to-- to Organization Todt, to Spain.
I say, what's his name?
Biedermann.
I said, that is a very unusual name.
I never heard that name.
Biedermann, in German, is the same like 'gentleman' in English.
And I say, isn't it strange?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The editor of the German newspaper in Holland, his name is Biedermann.
This may be his son.
Oh, Eric, you're crazy.
Let me talk to him.
I talked to him and I said to him, are you the son of the Biedermann of the He said, yes.
I-- OK.
I went to the office of the German newspaper in der Niederland-- in the Netherlands.
And I said to the receptionist, I'd like to talk with Mr.
Biedermann.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And she said to me, you can't talk to him.
You have no appointment.
I say, I come directly from Reichminister Dr.
Goebbels.
I have to talk to the editor of the newspaper.
And you have to take it upon your responsibility to sabotage it.
What's the matter with you?
I talked to Mr.
Biedermann.
And I said to him, do you remember your son, Rolf?
No answer.
No answer.
I say, well, anyway, let me be brief.
Your son deserted from the army.
He is right now under the protection of the Dutch underground.
We want to help him.
But you also have to help us.
I would like to have from you five press cards with your signature stamped-- name, blank.
It's very important to us.
No answer.
Said to him, well, you can call now the Gestapo and have me arrested, and I will be executed.
But if I am not back within four or five hours, so will your son.
No answer.
He picked up the phone.
I have no idea whom he is calling-- the Gestapo or his mistress or his secretary.
Called the secretary.
Bring me 10-- not 10-- three or four, I forgot-- three or four press cards.
Came in.
He signed them.
He gave them to me.
I said, thank you very much.
But he didn't say one word.
I left.
That man has never said one word.
I am personally convinced that he knew all about it, because he didn't act surprised.
There was a very other interesting German person.
Her name was Magdalena Sortmann.
She was the daughter of a minister from Lubeck, in Germany.
She had an antique bookshop in Amsterdam, and I knew her through the parents of my fiance.
And her bookshop-- people came there around and browsed and buy books, like an antique bookshop-- was the meeting place of many of the underground people.
Because, if I wanted to have a date with somebody, I said, you come to the bookshop.
She or he came there.
So did I.
We were looking at books and we would start talking about books.
And she did a tremendous amount of things.
After the war, I found out that she also had a Jewish man in her house during the war, to-- to hide him.
And her bookshop was the communications center.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Many-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What was my basic activity?
I had-- stop?
No.
No, it's fine.
I had five different addresses.
I had five different-- not apartments-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] furnished rooms.
I gave shelter to many people who came.
There were perhaps many times that underground-- people underground, Jews and non-Jews-- they were in-- lived somewhere in Holland.
They had to come to Amsterdam for two or three days, for one reason or another.
They stayed with me.
That was one of my activities.
The other activity I had is finding hiding places.
And the other very important activity, which doesn't seem to be important, is most Jews who were underground were in hiding.
They never left their-- their hiding place.
Now, I was their eyes and ears to the world.
Furthermore, I got money.

The-- the Dutch government in exile, in London, made, through some wealthy people in Holland, money available to be distributed to underground Jews, because many of the families who took them in, they could not afford to feed them.
And they had no ration cards.
So I had to bring them money.
I brought them books.
I brought them newspapers.
I had to play chess with them.
I had to play cards with them.
And I was the eyes and ears to the world for dozens of people.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And as long as they were alive, it's good.
But what happens if an old Jewish man of 68 dies, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a home where he lives underground?
What happens?
You cannot call a beauty-- a-- a funeral parlor.
Right.
You cannot put him into a sack over your shoulder and throw him in-- into one of the canals in Holland.
Because you might be arrested for black marketing.
What we have in there.
You know what you have to do?

You have to put him into a bathtub and deplete him-- cut him-- deplete him, and then cut him in little pieces, and then take him out, 15 or 20 times, 8 to 10 pound at a time, in a little saucepan, and throw him in the canal.
That's also what I had to do.
I didn't like it.
But how do you get rid of an underground, 68-year-old, Jewish body?
That's the way to do it.
What about--
How did I make a living?
I was selling cos-- cosmetic things to-- to-- to stores, to barbershops and hairdressers.
From a-- very, very interesting.
From a wholesaler.
The name of the place was [?
Vendita, ?] on the [INAUDIBLE].
And he-- he gave it to me, and I was peddling it for a profit.
And that's the way I made the living.
And, I may add, a very good living.
I made lots of money.
I mean, lots of money.
I made money so that I could live very, very well and I could take care of many other people with the money I made.
In-- in selling cosmetics.
So it was in between.
I mean, I could go on and on about many different things.
But I think that is the gist of everything.
No.
[NON-ENGLISH] 8.
[NON-ENGLISH] 121.
[NON-ENGLISH] 14.
[NON-ENGLISH] 14.
Just to give some addresses.
All in-- all different parts of Amsterdam.
I was living with myself and the people whom I took in and gave them an opportunity to sleep.
One very, very important thing happened.
Very important.
[NON-ENGLISH] number 8, I had a room there with a huge bed.
That bed could sleep eight people, easily.
And that was a place where many people slept.
Many times, girls and boys together.
Many times, I slept with one girl only.
Never any idea of sex or touching.
It was out of the question.
And I knew very well a Jewish boy-- man-- he was a man from Vienna.
His name was Karli Oruslan.
He was working for the [?
Expositour. ?] The [?
Expositour ?] was the Department of the Federation Council-- the Jewish council-- who was working together with the Gestapo.
And therefore he was not subject to deportation.
He wore a yellow star.
But he didn't want to go home at night, sometimes, because he felt they still may pick him up.
So he sometimes came sleeping with me.
